title: Handling Returns Under GDP
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: A senior QP's practical guide to GDP returns: quarantine, storage-condition evidence, cold-chain rules and the documented decisions MHRA inspectors expect.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury pharmaceutical banner in a cool teal and cyan palette over deep navy, with a single subtle green accent. A sealed, unbranded medicine carton and a small temperature-data logger rest on a brushed-steel quarantine shelf in a pristine distribution facility, gently bathed in soft natural side light. Shallow depth of field throws blurred warehouse racking and a faint cold-store door into a calm bokeh background, conveying control, traceability and clinical precision. 4:3 aspect ratio, no text, no logos, no readable labels.

faqs.q: Can a returned medicine always be put back into saleable stock under GDP?
faqs.a: No. Under the EU GDP guidelines the default is quarantine, and stock may only be returned to saleable inventory when defined conditions are met. These include intact original packaging, satisfactory evidence of approved storage conditions, acceptable remaining shelf life and a documented assessment by an authorised person. If any doubt about quality remains, the product must not be re-issued.

faqs.q: What happens to cold-chain products returned without temperature data?
faqs.a: Treat the absence of continuous monitoring data as a fail. Without objective evidence that a temperature-sensitive product stayed within its labelled storage conditions throughout the time it was out of your control, it should not be returned to saleable stock. Any recorded excursions should be assessed against the manufacturer's stability data before disposition, and where doubt persists the product should be destroyed.

faqs.q: Who is allowed to decide that a returned product can be resold?
faqs.a: The disposition decision is a quality judgement, not a warehouse task. It must be made by a person who is specifically trained and authorised to do so, working under the oversight of the Responsible Person and against defined acceptance criteria. Inspectors expect to see documented training, delegated authority and a recorded rationale for each decision.
